questions.passage: Portable video game consoles and wireless speakers tend to contain batteries that can't be easily __________. Environmental policy researcher Jessika Richter warns that because new batteries can't be put in once the old ones no longer work, the gadgets stop functioning and are usually disposed of as trash.
questions.questionText: Which choice completes the text with the most logical and precise word or phrase?

choices.text: rated
invented
prevented
replaced

questions.passage: Whether Carmen Lomas Garza is creating small paintings and illustrations or large public artworks—such as Baile, a copper cutout of traditional Mexican dance in the San Francisco International Airport—she is __________ direct experience, drawing from memories of her childhood in Texas or details of her current surroundings in California.
questions.questionText: Which choice completes the text with the most logical and precise word or phrase?

choices.text: complimented by
uncertain about
unbothered by
inspired by

questions.passage: Domesticated thousands of years ago by Indigenous people in South America, the avocado deviates structurally from the wild plant it is descended from. Summer squash, another crop domesticated by Indigenous Americans, shows so little resemblance to any wild plant that genetic research was necessary to __________ the wild Johnny gourd as its ancestor.
questions.questionText: Which choice completes the text with the most logical and precise word or phrase?

choices.text: confirm
attract
develop
acquire

questions.passage: When considering which plays and musicals to produce, theaters in New York often favor keeping audiences happy over taking risks. So while they might be eager to produce an established classic like Annie, for example, most would be __________ to stage a work from a relatively unknown playwright.
questions.questionText: Which choice completes the text with the most logical and precise word or phrase?

choices.text: fortunate
hesitant
determined
surprised

questions.passage: When classical pianist Martha Argerich performs, it appears as if the music is coming to her spontaneously. She's highly skilled technically, but because of how freely she plays and her willingness to take risks, she seems relaxed and natural. Her apparent ease, however, is due to a tremendous amount of preparation. Despite Argerich's experience and virtuosity, she never takes for granted that she knows a piece of music. Instead, she approaches the music as if encountering it for the first time and tries to understand it anew.
questions.questionText: Which choice best states the main purpose of the text?

choices.text: To provide details about how Argerich identifies which pieces of music she will perform
To assert that Argerich's performances look effortless because of how she prepares for them
To discuss the kinds of music Argerich feels most comfortable encountering for the first time
To describe the unique way that Argerich approaches music she hasn't performed before

questions.passage: Dubautia linearis is a species in a family of plants known collectively as the silversword alliance, all of which grow only on the Hawaiian Islands. Members of this alliance exhibit an extraordinary range of phenotypes, with some species maturing into vines and others into shrubs and trees. All species in the alliance descended from a single ancestral tarweed plant that arrived on the islands around 5 million years ago. The tarweed's descendants diversified into distinct species as they adapted to live in the wide variety of habitats found on the Hawaiian Islands.
questions.questionText: Which choice best describes the function of the underlined phrase in the text as a whole?

choices.text: It lists species from a family of plants found only on the Hawaiian Islands.
It provides the common name for the Dubautia linearis plant.
It supplies the name used to refer to a group of related plant species.
It explains why Dubautia linearis is unique among members of the silversword alliance.

questions.passage:
The following text is adapted from Jerome K. Jerome's 1889 novel Three Men in a Boat (To Say Nothing of the Dog).

We [people] are creatures of the sun. We love light and life. That is why we crowd into the towns and cities, and the country grows more and more deserted every year. In the sunlight—in the daytime, when Nature is alive and busy all around us, we like the open hill-sides and the deep woods well enough: but in the night, when our Mother Earth has gone to sleep, and left us waking, oh! the world seems so lonesome, and we get frightened, like children in a silent house. Then we sit and sob, and long for the gas-lit streets, and the sound of human voices, and the answering throb of human life.
questions.questionText: Which choice best states the main purpose of the text?

choices.text: To address common traits that motivate many people to choose to live in urban environments
To demonstrate that most people tend to prefer being alone in the country to being alone in towns or cities
To explain that people sometimes become fearful as a result of being in crowded areas
To caution people not to be so quick to dismiss the natural beauty that can be found in rural areas

questions.passage:
Text 1
According to a study by a conservation group representing 11 tribal nations in the Great Lakes region, the snowshoe hare (waabooz in the Ojibwe language) will have significantly worse outcomes over the next 50 years if temperatures increase as much as some models suggest. By contrast, the American crow (aandeg in Ojibwe) should be able to withstand the highest predicted warming without much harm and so likely won't require the conservation efforts that the snowshoe hare will.

Text 2
US government agencies involved in conservation are unfortunately not able to address every possible threat to natural resources. They must use the best information available to decide which species are most threatened and therefore most in need of conservation efforts.
questions.questionText: Based on the texts, both authors would most likely agree with which statement?

choices.text: State, federal, and tribal groups involved in natural-resource management in the Great Lakes region should immediately begin conservation programs for both the snowshoe hare and American crow.
A collaborative approach is necessary to keep temperatures in the Great Lakes region from increasing to the highest predicted levels.
Agencies involved in natural-resource management in the Great Lakes region should focus their conservation efforts more on the snowshoe hare than on the American crow.

Conservation efforts focused on the snowshoe hare are more likely to be successful if they incorporate state and federal agency resources with the knowledge of tribal groups in the Great Lakes region.

questions.passage: The ancient writing system used in the Maya kingdoms of southern Mexico and Central America had a symbol for the number zero. The earliest known example of the symbol dates to more than 2,000 years ago. At that time, almost none of the writing systems elsewhere in the world possessed a zero symbol. And the use of zero in Mexico and Central America may be even more ancient. Some historians suggest that Maya mathematicians inherited it from the Olmec civilization, which flourished in the region 2,400-3,600 years ago.
questions.questionText: According to the text, what do some historians suggest about Maya civilization?

choices.text: Maya civilization acquired the use of zero from the Olmec civilization.
Maya civilization respected its historians more than it respected its mathematicians.
Maya civilization was highly secretive about its intellectual achievements.
Maya civilization tried to introduce its writing system to other civilizations.

questions.passage: In El Salvador, use of solid fuel (e.g., coal, wood) as a share of total household fuel use fell by more than half between 2005 and 2015; such shifts are typically explained by appeal to the energy ladder, a model holding that fuel choice is mediated mainly by household income (specifically, high-technology fuels displace solid fuels as incomes rise). Richard Hosier and Jeffrey Dowd's study of fuel use in Zimbabwe shows how reductive this model is, however: household fuel use was heterogeneous, flexible, and influenced by several factors, including the local prices of different fuel sources.
questions.questionText: Based on the text, which choice best explains why the author characterizes the energy ladder as "reductive"?

choices.text: The energy ladder asserts that households only consider one aspect of fuel when making fuel choices, but Hosier and Dowd's study showed that households ignore that characteristic of fuel and evaluate several other factors instead.

The energy ladder assumes that the same factor influences household fuel choice in all locations, but Hosier and Dowd's study showed that fuel choice in Zimbabwe is influenced by different factors than is fuel choice in El Salvador.

The energy ladder holds that the adoption of one type of fuel means that another type of fuel must be displaced, but Hosier and Dowd's study showed that several different fuel types are typically used in equal proportion.
The energy ladder attributes household fuel choice primarily to a single characteristic of households, but Hosier and Dowd's study showed that multiple circumstances can affect household fuel choice.

questions.passage: "The Bet" is an 1889 short story by Anton Chekhov. In the story, a banker is described as being very upset about something:
questions.questionText: Which quotation from "The Bet" most effectively illustrates the claim?

choices.text: "Then the banker cautiously broke the seals off the door and put the key in the keyhole."
"It struck three o'clock, the banker listened; everyone was asleep in the house and nothing could be heard outside but the rustling of the chilled trees."
"The banker, spoilt and frivolous, with millions beyond his reckoning, was delighted at the bet."
"When [the banker] got home he lay on his bed, but his tears and emotion kept him for hours from sleeping."

questions.passage:
Some scientists have suggested that as ocean temperatures rise, many fish and corals found in the shallow zone (less than 30 meters below the surface) could take refuge in the cooler, darker mesophotic zone (30 to 150 meters below the surface). However, it isn't clear that all such species will be able to tolerate mesophotic conditions. In 2018, Luiz Rocha and colleagues studied stony corals in the two zones in Caribbean and Indo-Pacific waters. Based on the depths at which those corals are now found, the species that seems least suited to a full migration to the mesophotic zone is__________

Depth ranges of selected stony coral species:
- Cycloseris tenuis: Minimum 1m, Maximum 27m
- Acropora caroliniana: Minimum 10m, Maximum 25m
- Acropora anthocercis: Minimum 5m, Maximum 10m
- Agaricia fragilis: Minimum 10m, Maximum 102m
questions.questionText: Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to complete the statement?

choices.text: Acropora anthocercis, because its maximum depth of 10 meters is furthest from the mesophotic zone.
Cycloseris tenuis, because its minimum depth of 1 meter is the shallowest of those listed.
Agaricia fragilis, because its minimum depth of 10 meters is in the shallow zone.
Acropora caroliniana, because its maximum depth of 25 meters is close to but doesn't reach the mesophotic zone.

questions.passage: Water flowing around an obstruction creates vortices (patterns of swirls) of varying size; by detecting the vortices, fish can determine the size and position of the obstruction. Testing by Yuzo R. Yanagisuru, Otar Akanyeti, and James C. Liao using models of three head shapes—narrow (low ratio of width to length), intermediate, and wide (high ratio of width to length)—showed that for large vortices, fish with intermediate heads would be better able than narrow-headed fish to distinguish between vortices and general turbulence in the water. A second research team has therefore hypothesized that in low-visibility conditions, intermediate-headed fish will be more likely than narrow-headed fish to detect obstructions that create large vortices.
questions.questionText: Which finding, if true, would most directly support the second research team's hypothesis?

choices.text: A study using obstructions that created large vortices in low-visibility conditions found that the intermediate-headed Lobocheilos rhabdoura bumped into the obstructions just as often as the narrow-headed lookdown (Selene vomer).

A study using obstructions that created large vortices in low-visibility conditions found that the lookdown (Selene vomer), which has a relatively narrow head, bumped into more than half of the obstructions.

A study using obstructions that created large vortices in low-visibility conditions found that the narrow-headed lookdown (Selene vomer) bumped into obstructions more often than the intermediate-headed Lobocheilos rhabdoura did.

A study using obstructions that created large vortices in low-visibility conditions found that some specimens of the intermediate-headed Lobocheilos rhabdoura bumped into the obstructions more often than other specimens of the same fish did.

questions.passage:
In a college course on urban affairs, a student asserts that traffic congestion in the United States increased between 1990 and 2000 mostly in and around very large cities such as Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. The student claims that not only were smaller areas such as Albuquerque, New Mexico, and Anchorage, Alaska, less affected, but the amount of congestion in those areas remained constant throughout the same period. A second student claims that this is an unsupported assertion based on incorrect assumptions about traffic patterns in urban areas of different sizes.
[Graph shows Annual Average Hours of Highway Traffic Delay per Auto Commuter increasing for all three cities between 1990 and 2000]
questions.questionText: Which choice best describes data from the graph that support the second student's claim?

choices.text: In all three urban areas shown, the amount of traffic delay per commuter per year was greater in 2000 than in 1990.
Throughout the period between 1990 and 2000, the amount of traffic delay per commuter per year was greater in the Albuquerque, New Mexico, area than in the Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, area.
In at least one of the three urban areas shown, the amount of traffic delay was less than 20 hours per commuter per year at one point between 1990 and 2000.
In 1992, the amount of traffic delay in the Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, area was less than 30 hours per commuter per year.

questions.passage: Mariana Lopes Barata and Pedro Simoes Coelho collected data from 324 music-streaming service users to identify factors that influence users to opt for paid (premium) versions of music streaming services, like spotify. They hypothesized that effort expectancy (the perceived ease with which a service can be used by a consumer) would be positively correlated with users' intentions to adopt premium versions. The researchers asked participants to rate statements such as "I find paid music streaming services easy to use." They found that, indeed, effort expectancy positively influences the intention to adopt premium streaming service versions, which suggests that__________
questions.questionText: Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: it may be advantageous for music streaming companies to try to influence the extent to which potential users perceive the use of the premium version as effortless.
participants who strongly agreed with the statement "I find paid music streaming services easy to use" were more likely to express loyalty to Spotify than to other streaming services.
users' intentions to pay for premium streaming services are probably unaffected by factors other than effort expectancy.
the statement "I find paid music streaming services easy to use" is not a reliable measure of effort expectancy.

questions.passage: Technologies such as microphones and inkjet printers are made using piezoelectric materials, which generate an internal electric field when pressure is applied to them. The toxic nature of some of these materials recently led a team from the University of Sheffield to investigate how__________
questions.questionText: Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: could their use be better regulated?
their use could be better regulated.
their use could be better regulated?
could their use be better regulated.

questions.passage: Kayenta Chapter is one of the 110 chapters of the Navajo Nation (Naabeehó Bináhásdzo). The chapter, whose boundaries encompass 504,811 acres, __________ known as Tó Dínéeshzhee' in the Navajo language (Diné bizaad).
questions.questionText: Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: have been
is
are
were

questions.passage: While the greater adjutant can be found in places like the Inner Gulf of Thailand and the Chhep Wildlife Sanctuary in Cambodia, more than 80 percent of this endangered stork species is found in Assam, India. There, wildlife biologist Dr. Purnima Devi Barman is on the front lines of conservation efforts that—through community involvement and scientific __________ aim to bring adjutants back from near extinction.
questions.questionText: Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: study—
study:
study
study,

questions.passage: National Heritage Areas like the Oil Region National Heritage Area in Pennsylvania __________ officially designated by the United States Congress as being "unique [and] nationally significant" for their natural, cultural, and historical attributes.
questions.questionText: Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: has been
is
was
are

questions.passage: On March 23, 2021, a gust of wind wreaked havoc on global trade. Ever Given, an international shipping container vessel, became lodged in Egypt's Suez Canal, a major shipping route between Europe and Asia. The vessel took six days to __________ it's as heavy as two thousand blue whales when fully loaded.
questions.questionText: Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: dislodge in part due to its sheer size,
dislodge, in part due to its sheer size:
dislodge, in part due to its sheer size,
dislodge, in part, due to its sheer size

questions.passage: Just as the state of Massachusetts has a designated state flower, tree, and bird, it also has a state soil: Paxton soil. Paxton is a fitting choice for state soil, in large part because it can be found in abundance across the state. __________ it plays an important role in Massachusetts's agricultural industry and is used to grow a variety of crops, like corn and apples.
questions.questionText: Which choice completes the text with the most logical transition?

choices.text: At that time,
Additionally,
Alternatively,
By contrast,

questions.passage:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
• In the 1930s, the Imperial Sugar Cane Institute in India sought to limit the country's dependence on imported sugarcane.
• The institute enlisted botanist Janaki Ammal to breed a local variety of sugarcane.
• She crossbred the imported sugarcane species Saccharum officinarum with grasses native to India.
• She succeeded in creating sugarcane hybrids well suited to India's climate.
questions.questionText: The student wants to emphasize Janaki Ammal's achievement. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: By crossbreeding the imported sugarcane species Saccharum officinarum with grasses native to India, Ammal succeeded in creating sugarcane hybrids well suited to India's climate.
In the 1930s, the Imperial Sugar Cane Institute, which enlisted Ammal, sought to limit dependence on imported sugarcane.
Ammal was enlisted by the Imperial Sugar Cane Institute at a time when a local variety of sugarcane needed to be produced.
As part of efforts to breed a local variety of sugarcane, an imported sugarcane species called Saccharum officinarum was crossbred with grasses native to India.

questions.passage:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
• Richard Serra is an American artist.
• He is known for his large metal sculptures.
• His sculpture Rotate is roughly 6 feet wide and 12 feet tall.
• It is made from weathering steel.
questions.questionText: Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to emphasize the size of the sculpture?

choices.text: Richard Serra is an American artist known for his large metal sculptures.
Richard Serra's Rotate is made of weathering steel.
American artist Richard Serra creates large sculptures from metals such as weathering steel.
Rotate—a large metal sculpture by artist Richard Serra—stretches roughly 6 feet wide and 12 feet tall.

questions.passage:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
• Dinosaur fossil specimens can be found at science museums all over the world.
• A dinosaur fossil specimen nicknamed Jane is housed at the Burpee Museum of Natural History in Rockford, Illinois.
• Jane, a Tyrannosaurus, lived in the Late Cretaceous period.
• A dinosaur fossil specimen nicknamed Raymond is housed at the National Museum of Nature and Science in Tokyo, Japan.
• Raymond, a Triceratops, lived in the Late Cretaceous period.
questions.questionText: The student wants to emphasize a similarity between the two dinosaur fossil specimens. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: While the Tyrannosaurus fossil specimen Jane is in Rockford, Illinois, the Triceratops fossil specimen Raymond is in Tokyo, Japan.
Dinosaur fossil specimens can be found at science museums all over the world, including in Rockford, Illinois, and Tokyo, Japan.
The Burpee Museum of Natural History is home to Jane, a Tyrannosaurus fossil specimen from the Late Cretaceous period.
Both the Tyrannosaurus fossil specimen Jane and the Triceratops fossil specimen Raymond lived in the Late Cretaceous period.

questions.passage:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
• The Future of Nostalgia is a scholarly book by literary theorist Svetlana Boym.
• The book explores the concept of nostalgia from various angles.
• Chapter 6 explores nostalgia after the fall of the Soviet Union.
• Chapter 12 explores conceptions of nostalgia among émigrés.
• In chapter 12, Boym writes, "The main feature of exile is a double conscience, a double exposure of different times and spaces, a constant bifurcation."
questions.questionText: The student wants to explain what Svetlana Boym writes about in chapter 6. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Literary theorist Svetlana Boym explores nostalgia after the fall of the Soviet Union in chapter 6 of her book.
Svetlana Boym's The Future of Nostalgia explores the concept of nostalgia from various angles.
After exploring nostalgia after the fall of the Soviet Union, Svetlana Boym goes on to explore conceptions of nostalgia among emigres.
As Svetlana Boym writes in the twelfth chapter of her book, "The main feature of exile is a double conscience, a double exposure of different times and spaces, a constant bifurcation."

questions.passage:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
• The poem "15 haiku (for Toni Morrison)" is by African American writer Sonia Sanchez.
• It was published in her 2010 poetry book entitled Morning Haiku.
• The poem is written as a sequence of fifteen haiku.
• According to the book's publisher, Penguin Random House (PRH), the book "celebrates the gifts of life and mourns the deaths of revered African American figures."
• According to Sanchez, she chose to write in the form of haiku because it helps "maintain memory and dignity."
questions.questionText: The student wants to provide an example of a poem from Morning Haiku. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: The 2010 poetry book Morning Haiku "celebrates the gifts of life and mourns the deaths of revered African American figures."
The poem by Sanchez is written as a sequence of fifteen haiku.
Sanchez chose to write in the form of the haiku because it helps "maintain memory and dignity."
Included in Sanchez's poetry book Morning Haiku (2010), "15 haiku (for Toni Morrison)" is a poem written as a sequence of fifteen haiku.

questions.passage:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
• A river's stream length is the length of a line that follows along the river path.
• A river's valley length is the length of a straight line drawn from the beginning of the river to the end.
• The river's curviness, or sinuosity, is the ratio of the river's stream length to its valley length.
• This ratio is less than or equal to 1.5:1 in low-sinuosity rivers and greater than 1.5:1 in high-sinuosity rivers.
• The Red River in Canada has high sinuosity.
• The Neales River in Australia has low sinuosity.
questions.questionText: The student wants to compare the sinuosity of the Red and Neales rivers. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: While the high-sinuosity Red River is located in Canada, the Neales River is in Australia.
By comparing the stream lengths of the Red and Neales rivers to their valley lengths, one can calculate their sinuosity.
The ratio of the Red River's stream length to its valley length is greater than 1.5:1, exceeding that of the Neales River.
In both the Red and the Neales rivers, sinuosity is determined by comparing stream length to valley length.
